category.text: $course$/Advanced Algebra (M.Sc. Mathematics)/CSIR PYQ — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q1 — Group of order 15

questiontext.text: A group of order \(15\) is necessarily:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Cyclic.
Since \(|G|=15=3\times 5\), by Sylow's third theorem \(n_3\mid 5\) and \(n_3\equiv 1\pmod{3}\), so \(n_3=1\). Similarly \(n_5\mid 3\) and \(n_5\equiv 1\pmod{5}\), so \(n_5=1\). Both Sylow subgroups are normal, hence \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\times\mathbb{Z}_5\cong\mathbb{Z}_{15}\), which is cyclic.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q2 — Group of order p²

questiontext.text: Let \(p\) be a prime. Every group of order \(p^2\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Abelian.
For a group \(G\) of order \(p^2\), the center \(Z(G)\) is non-trivial (class equation). If \(|Z(G)|=p^2\), then \(G\) is abelian. If \(|Z(G)|=p\), then \(G/Z(G)\) has order \(p\), hence cyclic, which forces \(G\) to be abelian — a contradiction. So \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_{p^2}\) or \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_p\times\mathbb{Z}_p\), both abelian.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q3 — Center of a p-group

questiontext.text: If \(G\) is a finite group of order \(p^n\) (\(p\) prime, \(n\ge 1\)), then the center \(Z(G)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Non-trivial.
By the class equation \(|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum[G:C_G(x_i)]\), each term \([G:C_G(x_i)]\) is a power of \(p\) greater than 1. Since \(p\mid|G|\) and \(p\) divides each summand, we get \(p\mid|Z(G)|\), so \(Z(G)\ne\{e\}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q4 — Sylow 3-subgroups of S₄

questiontext.text: The number of Sylow \(3\)-subgroups of the symmetric group \(S_4\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 4.
\(|S_4|=24=2^3\cdot 3\). By Sylow's third theorem, \(n_3\mid 8\) and \(n_3\equiv 1\pmod{3}\), so \(n_3\in\{1,4\}\). The 3-cycles in \(S_4\) are \((123),(132),(124),(142),(134),(143),(234),(243)\) — 8 elements of order 3 forming 4 cyclic groups of order 3. Hence \(n_3=4\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q5 — Sylow 2-subgroups of S₃

questiontext.text: The number of Sylow \(2\)-subgroups of the symmetric group \(S_3\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 3.
\(|S_3|=6=2\cdot 3\). Sylow 2-subgroups have order 2. By Sylow's third theorem \(n_2\mid 3\) and \(n_2\equiv 1\pmod{2}\), so \(n_2\in\{1,3\}\). The three transpositions \((12),(13),(23)\) each generate a distinct subgroup of order 2, so \(n_2=3\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q6 — Sylow's Third Theorem

questiontext.text: If \(p^k\) is the highest power of prime \(p\) dividing \(|G|\), then the number \(n_p\) of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(n_p\equiv 1\pmod{p}\) and \(n_p\mid\frac{|G|}{p^k}\).
This is the content of Sylow's Third Theorem. The two conditions together heavily restrict possible values of \(n_p\) and are the main tools for proving groups of specific orders are not simple.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q7 — Sylow's Second Theorem

questiontext.text: Sylow's Second Theorem states that any two Sylow \(p\)-subgroups of a finite group \(G\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Conjugate in \(G\).
If \(P\) and \(Q\) are Sylow \(p\)-subgroups, then \(Q=gPg^{-1}\) for some \(g\in G\). In particular all Sylow \(p\)-subgroups are isomorphic. A corollary: a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup is normal iff \(n_p=1\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q8 — Group of order 35

questiontext.text: Every group of order \(35 = 5\times 7\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Cyclic.
\(n_5\mid 7\) and \(n_5\equiv 1\pmod{5}\Rightarrow n_5=1\). \(n_7\mid 5\) and \(n_7\equiv 1\pmod{7}\Rightarrow n_7=1\). Both Sylow subgroups are normal. Since \(\gcd(5,7)=1\), we get \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_5\times\mathbb{Z}_7\cong\mathbb{Z}_{35}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q9 — Groups of order 2p

questiontext.text: Let \(p\) be an odd prime. A group of order \(2p\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Either \(\mathbb{Z}_{2p}\) or \(D_p\).
By Sylow, \(n_p\mid 2\) and \(n_p\equiv 1\pmod p\), so \(n_p=1\). The Sylow \(p\)-subgroup \(P\cong\mathbb{Z}_p\) is normal. The Sylow 2-subgroup \(Q\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\) acts on \(P\) by conjugation. If the action is trivial, \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_{2p}\); if non-trivial (inversion), \(G\cong D_p\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q10 — Center of Q₈

questiontext.text: The center of the quaternion group \(Q_8=\{\pm 1,\pm i,\pm j,\pm k\}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\{1,-1\}\).
The elements \(i,j,k,-i,-j,-k\) do not commute with each other (e.g., \(ij=k\ne-k=ji\)). Only \(\pm 1\) commute with every element of \(Q_8\), so \(Z(Q_8)=\{1,-1\}\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q11 — Commutator subgroup of S₃

questiontext.text: The commutator (derived) subgroup \([S_3, S_3]\) of the symmetric group \(S_3\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}_3\).
\([S_3,S_3]=A_3=\{e,(123),(132)\}\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\). This is because \(S_3/A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\) is abelian, and \(A_3\) is the smallest normal subgroup with abelian quotient.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q12 — Commutator subgroup of S₄

questiontext.text: The derived subgroup \([S_4,S_4]\) of \(S_4\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(A_4\).
\(S_4/A_4\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\) is abelian, so \([S_4,S_4]\subseteq A_4\). The quotient \(A_4/V_4\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\) is abelian, but \(S_4/V_4\cong S_3\) is non-abelian, confirming that \([S_4,S_4]=A_4\) of order 12.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q13 — G/Z(G) cyclic implies G abelian

questiontext.text: If \(G/Z(G)\) is cyclic, then \(G\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Abelian.
Suppose \(G/Z(G)=\langle gZ(G)\rangle\). Every element of \(G\) has the form \(g^m z\) for some \(m\in\mathbb{Z}, z\in Z(G)\). Then \((g^m z_1)(g^n z_2)=g^{m+n}z_1z_2=(g^n z_2)(g^m z_1)\), so \(G\) is abelian. But then \(G=Z(G)\), making \(G/Z(G)\) trivial (cyclic of order 1).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q14 — A₅ is not solvable

questiontext.text: Which of the following groups is NOT solvable?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(A_5\).
\(A_5\) is simple and non-abelian. A simple group is solvable if and only if it is cyclic of prime order. Since \(|A_5|=60\) is not prime, \(A_5\) is not solvable. \(S_3,S_4,A_4\) are all solvable (they have composition factors of prime order).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q15 — Jordan–Hölder Theorem

questiontext.text: The Jordan–Hölder theorem guarantees that any two composition series of a finite group \(G\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Have the same length and the same composition factors (up to isomorphism and ordering).
This is the uniqueness part of the Jordan–Hölder theorem. While composition series need not be identical, they always yield the same multiset of composition factors (simple groups appearing as successive quotients), analogous to unique prime factorisation.

answer.text: Have the same length and isomorphic composition factors (up to ordering)
Have the same length but may have different composition factors

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q16 — Solvable group and composition factors

questiontext.text: A finite group \(G\) is solvable if and only if all its composition factors are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Cyclic of prime order.
The composition factors of a solvable group are simple groups with a solvable group structure. The only solvable simple groups are the cyclic groups \(\mathbb{Z}_p\) for prime \(p\). Conversely, if all composition factors are \(\mathbb{Z}_p\), one constructs the derived series terminating at \(\{e\}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q17 — Nilpotent finite group

questiontext.text: A finite group \(G\) is nilpotent if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(G\) is the direct product of its Sylow subgroups.
The three equivalent characterisations of finite nilpotent groups are: (i) \(G\) is the direct product of its Sylow subgroups, (ii) every Sylow subgroup is normal in \(G\), (iii) every subgroup of \(G\) is subnormal.

answer.text: \(G\) is the direct product of its Sylow subgroups

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q18 — Lower central series

questiontext.text: The lower central series of a group \(G\) is defined by \(G_0=G\) and:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(G_{i+1}=[G,G_i]\).
The lower central series \(G=G_0\supseteq G_1\supseteq G_2\supseteq\cdots\) is defined by \(G_{i+1}=[G,G_i]\), where \([G,G_i]\) is generated by all commutators \([g,h]=ghg^{-1}h^{-1}\) with \(g\in G,h\in G_i\). \(G\) is nilpotent of class \(n\) iff \(G_n=\{e\}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q19 — Frattini subgroup

questiontext.text: The Frattini subgroup \(\Phi(G)\) of a finite group \(G\) is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The intersection of all maximal subgroups of \(G\).
\(\Phi(G)=\bigcap\{M:M\text{ maximal in }G\}\). An element \(x\in G\) belongs to \(\Phi(G)\) iff \(x\) is a non-generator of \(G\) (removing \(x\) from any generating set still leaves a generating set). For a \(p\)-group \(G\), \(\Phi(G)=G^p[G,G]\).

answer.text: The intersection of all maximal subgroups of \(G\)
The intersection of all normal subgroups of \(G\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q20 — Number of groups of order 8

questiontext.text: The number of non-isomorphic groups of order \(8\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 5.
The five groups of order 8 are: \(\mathbb{Z}_8\), \(\mathbb{Z}_4\times\mathbb{Z}_2\), \(\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_2\) (abelian), and \(D_4\) (dihedral), \(Q_8\) (quaternion) (non-abelian).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q21 — A₄ has no subgroup of order 6

questiontext.text: The alternating group \(A_4\) of order \(12\) has:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: No subgroup of order 6.
If \(H\le A_4\) with \(|H|=6\), then \([A_4:H]=2\), so \(H\trianglelefteq A_4\). But a subgroup of order 6 would contain a unique subgroup of order 3 (also normal in \(A_4\)), yet \(A_4\) has four subgroups of order 3 (generated by each 3-cycle), so no single one can be normal in \(A_4\) within \(H\). This is a contradiction.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q22 — Order of Aut(Zₚ)

questiontext.text: For a prime \(p\), the order of the automorphism group \(\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_p)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(p-1\).
An automorphism of \(\mathbb{Z}_p\) is determined by the image of the generator \(1\), which must be sent to any non-zero element. There are \(p-1\) choices, so \(|\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_p)|=p-1\). Indeed \(\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_p)\cong(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\cong\mathbb{Z}_{p-1}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q23 — Maximal subgroup of p-group

questiontext.text: Every maximal subgroup of a \(p\)-group \(G\) of order \(p^n\) (\(n\ge 1\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Normal of index \(p\) in \(G\).
If \(M\) is a maximal subgroup of \(G=p\)-group, then \(G/M\) has no proper non-trivial subgroups and is a \(p\)-group, so \(G/M\cong\mathbb{Z}_p\). Thus \([G:M]=p\) and since \(G/M\) is abelian, \(M\) contains \([G,G]\) and is automatically normal.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q24 — Class equation

questiontext.text: The class equation of a finite group \(G\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum_{i}[G:C_G(x_i)]\).
Here the sum runs over one representative \(x_i\) from each conjugacy class outside the center. Each \([G:C_G(x_i)]>1\). This equation is fundamental in proving the center of a \(p\)-group is non-trivial, as \(p\) divides \(|G|\) and each index term.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q25 — Sylow 7-subgroups of group of order 168

questiontext.text: The number of Sylow \(7\)-subgroups of a simple group of order \(168=2^3\cdot 3\cdot 7\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 8.
\(n_7\mid 24\) and \(n_7\equiv 1\pmod{7}\), so \(n_7\in\{1,8\}\). If \(n_7=1\), the unique Sylow 7-subgroup would be normal, contradicting simplicity. Hence \(n_7=8\). (The simple group of order 168 is \(\mathrm{PSL}(2,7)\).)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q26 — Composition series length of S₄

questiontext.text: The length of a composition series of \(S_4\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 4.
A composition series of \(S_4\) is \(\{e\}\trianglelefteq\langle(12)(34)\rangle\trianglelefteq V_4\trianglelefteq A_4\trianglelefteq S_4\) with composition factors \(\mathbb{Z}_2,\mathbb{Z}_2,\mathbb{Z}_3,\mathbb{Z}_2\). Length = number of factors = 4.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q27 — pq group (q not 1 mod p)

questiontext.text: Let \(p < q\) be primes with \(q\not\equiv 1\pmod{p}\). Then every group of order \(pq\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Cyclic.
By Sylow, \(n_p\mid q\) and \(n_p\equiv 1\pmod p\). Since \(q\not\equiv 1\pmod p\), we get \(n_p=1\). Similarly \(n_q\mid p\) and \(n_q\equiv 1\pmod q\), so \(n_q=1\). Both Sylow subgroups are normal and \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_p\times\mathbb{Z}_q\cong\mathbb{Z}_{pq}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q28 — Elements of order 2 in Dₙ (n odd)

questiontext.text: In the dihedral group \(D_n\) of order \(2n\), where \(n\) is odd, the number of elements of order \(2\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(n\).
\(D_n\) has \(n\) rotations and \(n\) reflections. When \(n\) is odd, each rotation \(r^k\) (\(k\ne 0\)) has odd order dividing \(n\), so no rotation (other than \(e\)) has order 2. Each of the \(n\) reflections has order 2. Therefore there are exactly \(n\) elements of order 2.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q29 — Characteristic subgroup

questiontext.text: Which of the following is always a characteristic subgroup of any group \(G\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The center \(Z(G)\).
A characteristic subgroup is invariant under all automorphisms of \(G\). Since any automorphism \(\phi\) maps the center to itself (\(\phi(Z(G))=Z(G)\), as \(\phi\) preserves commutativity), \(Z(G)\) is always characteristic. Similarly \([G,G]\) and \(\Phi(G)\) are characteristic.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit I Q30 — Derived series of A₅

questiontext.text: The derived series \(A_5\supset A_5^{(1)}\supset A_5^{(2)}\supset\cdots\) of \(A_5\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Stationary at \(A_5\) — it never reaches \(\{e\}\).
Since \(A_5\) is simple and non-abelian, its commutator subgroup \([A_5,A_5]=A_5\). Therefore every term of the derived series equals \(A_5\), confirming that \(A_5\) is not solvable.
